No worries.

It's a little crazy.

So trying to finish things off before, you know, things get a little wild.
And yeah, what about you?
We're doing good.
We launched the community like officially this week.
And now we have, I think we started the week at like 70 paying members.
And now we have, let me check, think we're like 140 something now.
Oh, wow.
So we already doubled it.
That's awesome.
And they're all paying us, too, which is the best part.
Thanks.
Thanks.

Yeah, so we're definitely like there's so much to do for it but let me think yeah I mean I think today I just wanted it to be like just like spend a little bit more time talking about stuff you've worked on in the past and again feel free to ask me anything about you know stuff we're doing at any time but just wanted to spend some time there and yeah and that's basically it just because we didn't have enough time last time so I think in general I guess the way we do this interview is just like just ask a bunch of questions you know like I mainly care about like the actual details of it I think by by results I think like everything you described before like makes a ton of sense but yeah I think just like you know usually getting in the weeds is like where stuff really happens so
So that's the goal of this.
Yeah.
Does that sound good with you?
Any questions before we start?
Yeah.
Sounds good to me.
So just to double check, like you want to hear like some tactical stuff, right?
Like how do I kind of.
Yeah.
And obviously just ask, but like just what to expect.
So, yeah.
So, I mean, if you could start, I mean, I feel like Mutiny has seemed super interesting.
I've actually heard of Procurify as well.
But just starting with Mutiny, like the most interesting thing you brought up last time was just like the virtual conference, I think.
Was that attached to Sastr or was that like a separate event?
Yeah.
That was a separate event that we did.
That was somewhat related to the general theme because like what we did at Mutiny was that we had like a, you know, narrative calendar for the quarter.
This quarter, we focus on this.
Next quarter, we focus on something else.
So yeah, that was an interesting year because that's when marketing budgets were cut across the board.
And so the narrative for that quarter was around essentially the second lever, like conversion, essentially.

That's what we were talking about, the second lever, where it's like, if you don't really have the budget to spend any more on top of funnel, how do you make sure you're getting higher convergence rates so that they actually book a demo, so that they actually turn it to pipeline?
So that was kind of like the narrative that was given to us.
And then so from a demand generation and community building perspective, we're thinking like, what are some of the programs that really get this out to the world?
And then the second lever, virtual conference, that was one of the things that we proposed.
So I led the efforts there by looking at like, what are some of the best CMO speakers that are kind of like already pioneering that sort of angle, right, at the time?
Great.
And just like, like stepping back a bit.
So it's a cool idea.
guess every quarter you have a theme.
How does that work?
How is that theme set?
then what is the team at Mutiny?
I know the founder was a CMO before, so it's kind of an interesting combination.
It's the same case here.
So is she the one that's setting the theme?
Are you involved in that at all?
And then once that theme is set, who are all the different teams that kind of try to execute on it?
Yeah, I think the team structure might actually be quite similar to you guys.
Correct me if I'm wrong, but basically we had ACMO turned CEO, founder.

And then what we would do is get together every quarter to actually do an offsite where we go over, here's what we want to plan for the quarter in terms of both theme and also product direction and also GTM strategy.
So the team was actually quite light.
So I led community.
There was another person that led content.
We didn't have a CMO per se.
We had like a VP marketing, which is very similar to your role.
He led like go-to-market in general.
And then we also had someone lead ABM.
So that's kind of like marketing.
Got it.
And then, okay, so you guys set the theme together at this offsite.
And then I guess community rolls up under just the marketing function.
And like, I guess the decision of what community is doing, because it feels like, like, was community of mutiny, like, very events centric?
Was there any other component to it?
Like, yeah, was there like a Slack or something like that?
Like, how did you guys even think about what community was responsible for?
Yeah.
So how we thought about community is almost like a funnel in itself, right?
So the start of like getting into the community of mutiny is like, one is the newsletter.
So the newsletter is one.
And part of the community, how we think about it.
So that's kind of like, if you think about it, top funnel.
And then after that, right, like they get to know about some of the events that we're doing, including the second lever conference, including like some of the meetups and dinners that we were doing.

And then afterwards, once they go and interact with other people who've been to those events and also some of our team members, they then get added into a Slack community, which we named it M2, which it's like M squared.
It stands for modern marketers, because the goal there was that we wanted to get a lot of like marketers who are thinking of the new way that doing marketing.
It's not just focused on top funnels, not just focused on brand, but how can you actually, you know, measure the results from your marketing, essentially like the second lever.
So that's kind of like the entire conversion funnel.

Once they're in the community, then essentially we also had opportunities to kind of like promote customers within the community to then teach them sessions on how to use both Mutiny, but also more like Thaw Leader Serp style sessions on essentially, you know, growing your pipeline, how to really get better conversion.
From landing pages, stuff like that.
So that's kind of like how we built it out.
And was it like that linear in practice where like you kind of have to go through the newsletter to then get into the Slack channel?
Or were they kind of almost like separate things with some people that didn't kind of overlap?
Yeah.
So the ideal process was like linear, but really how it worked is almost more like a flywheel.
So when I try to explain this to people, I try to say like it's like a funnel, except it's more like a flywheel.
So there's entry points from the newsletter into the community.
And then from the community, of course, we also got people to the events.
And then from the newsletter itself, right, like we sourced some insights from the community.
So that also fed that layer of content.
And then also when we're...
Like a survey or something, like a newsletter?
Or what you you source the insights?
Yeah.
So for example, like for example, we would start a thread in the community where it's like, let me think about.
Like, me
One that we did before in the past, like what's your biggest challenge when it comes to conversion this quarter, for example, right?
Like that was one prompt that we put and people kind of like responded.
And I think the good thing is that we started the community off with our most engaged customers and prospects.
So they're very vocal and they would write out like sometimes a few bullet points, sometimes a paragraph.
And then I would send that over to our head of content.
He would then put that into a community insight section of the newsletter where it's like, hey, like this quarter is all about, you know, challenges with conversion.
This month, like here's what our community members have said.

And then what he would do is then turn those insights into a blog post afterwards where it's like, here's our recommendations coming from Mutiny, like how you can really, I guess, fix that based on the problem.
So that was kind of like a whole loop there.
And then the other loop too is, you know, when we sent out the newsletter as well, we would also include the events in there.
And then we would say, hey, do you want to kind of have a say in how we build these events?
Come join the community.
And sometimes we would do something about this.
It's easy as like in the poll where it's like, hey, do you guys want pizza?
Do you guys want pasta?
And just have people vote.
So they're part of the experience.
Got it.
So you had like a head of content who managed the newsletter.
I guess what content was in the newsletter.
So I guess your main role, was it like more on the events side?
Or like how would you describe kind of what you were mostly like owning?
Yeah.
So we didn't have an events person.
So I was like the own Slack community, I guess, moderator slash admin slash program builder.
And then also on, you know, the events side, like I was the person kind of coordinating the events and making sure working with sales to do follow-ups afterwards.
And then the third piece that was not an official role per se, but it was more like my day-to-day was also customer marketing.
So when we really talked to an amazing customer in the community, or if we met them at an event, I'm getting- So.
That into our case study process, essentially.

So that's like a partnership between our head of content, where if I notice, like, hey, there's a really great story here to be told, like, let's try to get them, you know, told in a more playbook format.
And then that would be something that I work with him on.
Got it.
So then I guess, like, if the newsletter, so I guess it's a couple of entryways into, like, the community.
Like, just at the newsletter level, like, how did you grow that?
Like, how did the newsletter get more subscribers?
Were there any initiatives to kind of, you know, like, grow that subscriber base, essentially?
Yeah.
So our head of content, one thing that he was responsible for is overall traffic to the new website.
And so, like, part of that was around, like, SEO, right?
And we also did paid ads as well, but they were not really just keyword driven, but it was more around also, like, display ads.
So we.
We used a lot of like the customer content, like the case studies, the playbooks, and then we displayed them on LinkedIn.
I think we also ran, what was it?
Like, I don't remember which platform it was on, but it wasn't just LinkedIn ads.
It might have been also like Google or something like that.
So that was also another way that we got like net new traffic.
Another way too is that when we, you know, created content in conjunction with our community members and customers, right?
We would also get content from them sharing as well.
So sometimes when we approach the company, when it comes to like a speaking opportunity at an event or a community session, we also look at like their audience.

Cause you know, like if they have a large following or if they have a self-stack newsletter or like a newsletter of some sort, then essentially, you know, we can kind of leverage both like our audience and their audience.
Yeah.
All right.
That makes sense.
I guess just getting into the, okay.
So you, so you have content that drives people, I guess.
It's the newsletter.
And then the newsletter, I guess, drives people to the community.
And then the community seems to be like the core things there are events and the Slack group.
Is that accurate, I guess?
Yeah, so we got like the in-person events, which is one is our virtual conference.
that's kind of like a bigger scale.
And then we've got like the in-person type events.
So there's a bigger ones where there's like the CMO meetup I mentioned last time at HubSpot.
Sorry, not HubSpot, Saster.
And then we also got more localized events, right?
Like San Francisco meetup, New York meetup, et cetera.
And then we also had like online sessions, which we branded conversion secrets.
So this is more like very similar to what you guys do, almost like master classes, except it's smaller in scale.
We would bring in like one customer or one influencer who knows the product.
bring customer.
They come in, they kind of essentially teach people how to really use Mutiny, and then it's like a step-by-step.

So then they teach people that, we have a Q&A afterwards, and then what we also do is we essentially build that playbook out in the content, so then people can read it as well if they couldn't attend the event.
That's cool.
Yeah, I mean, I've been trying to think about that.
So like the partnership piece to create content, like what did that look like?
Like, just could you describe that whole operation?
Like finding the right partners or?
Well, I guess all of it.
So it sounds like, I mean, there's, I guess, a piece of it that involves like, how do you find and get somebody on board to even do this with you?
And then once they agree, like, what does that even mean?
Like, how do you create the content, get people to that session?
How do you run that session?
What do you do after?
Or like, just that whole kind of life cycle, could you explain how that works?
Yeah, for sure.
Like, what do you do you do?
For like the online, I guess, like summit or like that online conference that we did, right?
That one was not just limited to working with customers for the speakers, right?
Like for that one, we really wanted to, the lever was really like reach.
We wanted as many people as possible that were within our ICP.
So we were like, okay, we want to have customer sessions, but we also want to have like a few keynote speakers that also could be the draw for why people wanted to come.
So that was very different from like the smaller events where we focus a lot on, you know, the customer playbooks on why people should use Mutiny and like the value they got from it.
So the second lever of that one was a little bit of both.
So we first started off with like in our VC network, like what are some, you know, VCs who can give us introductions to really amazing CMOs.

And then we also took a look at our target accounts because we had like an ABM strategy where every quarter we would, you know, like take a look at who are the customers essentially that we would love to close.
And then that was.
So also a way where we would build a relationship with the potential customer, like even though they're not a customer yet, like we bring them in as a speaker, right?
So we build that relationship.
And then afterwards, when we did one of these like bigger events, we would also do a private executive dinner afterwards with our CEO would be there.
And then sometimes like our VP of sales would be there as well.
So then it becomes almost like a nurture game where it goes from relationship to like trying to convert them later down the line.
So just on the virtual conference, guess, like you said, you went through the VCs, like what exactly was done there?
Like the founders talked to the VCs to get you a list that you would message or like, like, like what had to happen?
And what did you do specifically like to get that person to say like, hey, cool, I'll come, I'll do a fireside chat, I'll do a workshop at the conference.
So this may sound unconventional, but we actually created an alias email for our CEO.
So.
So.
Like it looked like it came from Jolly, but technically I'm the one sending out the emails.
So it would be like a personal favor type email where it's like, hey, you know, we're, our team is doing this conference that speaks on A, B, and C.
Like we think you would be an amazing speaker based on, you know, past experiences.
We would do some research on, you know, if they've spoken about that topic in the past and like backlink that.
And then essentially we would ask to see if this is something that you'd be open to, like feel free to loop in, you know, your PR team or content team.
So we work on that together.
And then if they say yes afterwards, then it sounds kind of unconventional, but then using that email, she or myself would loop myself in.
And then we would then work with them to kind of like get them on stage.
That makes sense.
And like what would exactly sell them to say yes to doing it?
If you tell them about the reach, do you pay them?
Like why, why would people agree?
Yeah, so we would try to get a few speakers.
These are customers that we have a really good relationship with.
So then we would name drop the people who are already interested.
also sometimes when we haven't locked in the keynote speakers, we already start promoting.
So we can already say there's already 500 people signed up and most people are senior manager levels and up.
And they want to hear from senior leaders like yourself.
And then we would also say one person that already expressed interest is the CMO of Box and the CMO of Salesforce.
I want to ask whether you would be also interested because we have like a last slot available.
Please let us know by X date.
So that adds like a little bit of urgency as well.
Okay.
That makes sense.
And was this the same kind of playbook for the smaller sessions or just like one, like an influencer, for example, doing a session with you guys?
Yeah.
So for the bigger sessions, it was more of like this play that I'm talking about for the smaller ones.
Those ones are.

So what we would do is look at like some of the accounts underneath our customer portfolio, and then we would target the ones that are like essentially has a really big brand name or reach or like a really cool case study, to be honest, right?
And then we would try to get them on stage.
And then from there too, sometimes what we would do is find out, for example, if they're in the ed tech space or if they're like a B2B sales company, right?
We'd find like essentially their competitors to then sell to, because, you know, there's like a audience match there.
And then we would also try to get them into the community that way, where it's like, hey, see how your competitor Snowflake, for example, is using Mutiny to convert customers.
Got it.
I don't remember how much Mutiny costs, but like was the community full of like people that were customers or was like...
Like how do the people in the community feel?
Like did it feel just like inherently valid?
Valuable or only valuable if you were like a Mutiny customer and it was almost like enablement in some sense?
It was definitely more on the enablement side.
So when we started the community, it was started from our customers, but we try to make sure that it was not just focused on the product itself.
Because to be honest, it wouldn't be the right strategy because Mutiny was not like a PLG led company.
Like there was a barrier to entry to actually like use it and buy it.
It was not very cheap either.
So we started off with more of like this idea of, yeah, like getting marketers to essentially be more modern.
And by being more modern, like they think about the numbers, they think about conversion.
It's not just about like brand or copy or stuff like that.

So that's kind of like the essence of the community is that we started off with customers, but we expanded it to anyone who wants to learn how to be a modern marketer that they can join the community.
So it's very different from the community that I built here at Apollo, where it's really driven around the product because it's PLG led.
There's a lot of like free users that...
There's like expansion opportunity there.
So it's a little bit of a different thinking.
Makes sense.
So I guess like by the time it became Modern Marketer, I guess by the end of it, like how big was the community?
Yeah.
So the Modern Marketers community, let me see.
I think I might still be in it just as a lurker when I left the company.
We're at 3,000.
So that one, we launched from scratch.
So they're around like 2,300 right now or yeah, 2,300.
And that's the Slack group?
Yeah, exactly.
I'm not sure about the newsletter numbers or anything like that.
Yeah, I don't have access to that anymore.
Okay.
But I guess while you were there, was the newsletter like much more top of funnel and much bigger or was it like roughly the same size as the community?
It's a little bit bigger only because not everybody who reads the newsletter will join the community.
So there's a little bit of a drop off there.
of drop there.
So
We try our best to try to get people from the newsletter into the community, but not everyone would join.
So one extra step we also did too was just to explain like what you can get from the community a lot better.
So we actually have a community landing page as well where, you know, people can kind of read a little bit more about like what you get.
And then there's a form that launches you into Slack, essentially, very similar to what you guys have.
Got it.
And like, did you guys, like, what is the just like experience of a community member?
Like, what you say?
Just like, did you have kind of like a vision for what it meant to be in this community in terms of like what their journey would be, what they would do and how they would get value out of it?
Yeah.
So we plotted out very similar to like a customer journey.
There was like a community journey.
So as soon as people join right in Slack, you know, they got a series of onboarding emails and then they also got.
So like a welcome message coming from me.
So there's automations you can build in Slack.
And I'm sure you can do that too on an apps like Circle and whatnot.
But yeah, so we got an automation coming from me, like saying like, hey, welcome to the community.
Here's like, you know, a channel directory.
Here's like ways you can get started.
And then we also add them to a few default channels.
So one is like obviously introductions channel.
The other one's like quick resources.
So that's where we would put a lot of our upcoming events, content, and playbooks.
So that's very similar, I think, what you guys do, except I know you guys don't host on Slack.
But yeah, just like essentially a landing page for when they get into Slack.
And then I would also check with them like one week later.
Like I would give them some onboarding tasks where it's like, make sure to use your real name, upload a photo.
So we know you're not a robot, right?
Like playing into that kind of thing.
Make sure to write your introductions.
And then after they've done all that, I say like, message me back.
And then we'll send you a piece of swag as well.
Just kind of like give you a warm welcome.
And then once they kind of, you know.
Do their thing in the community.
I send them another Slack message.
Again, this is automated through what we use, this tool called Common Room.
And then two weeks later, I check in with them.
Be like, hey, Jason, you know, you've been in the community for two weeks now.
Just want to like check in personally.
You know, how are you feeling?
How are you liking it?
Is there any, you know, immediate feedback that you have to make this like a more valuable place for you?
And so obviously it wasn't super scalable to export all of those comments into like a doc or anything like that.
But I did read through some of them because they're actually like really great ideas.
And I would implement them into, you know, like what are some of the programming we want to put for the next month and the quarters after.
Okay, that makes sense.
So like for Mutiny, like was there like a North Star for like a metric you were trying to drive?
Yeah, so there was two for the community.
So one was just like net new community members.
And the other one was around like engagement rate, which was essentially like the weekly active members divided by the total amount of members.
like there and just like So
How many of those people were actually like engaged and active?
So just on, I guess, new members, what were some of the, I guess, channels or like ways you tried to move that?
So one was through our events.
One was through our newsletter.
One was also through like our website.
So it was underneath like the H1 links.
So resources, it was joined the community.

The other one too is that like sales, they, when they're working an account, And if, you know, at a later stage, if they find that they're interested, but they're not ready to buy, they would say like, hey, you know, if you're not ready to buy, no worries.
Come join the M2 community.
You might get some inspiration from other people similar to you.
And then also CS would try to send them into the community right after they've onboarded to say like, here's the last piece of your onboarding.
Like come join the community.
can find other customers.
You can also find other people to learn from.
So then are each of those.
Cause like, I guess the newsletter, your head of content is like, I mean, that's the thing I imagine is like getting more organic traffic, you know, driving people to that audience in the newsletter.

Like what would your, like, what would an initiative that you kind of spearhead look like for each of those or for not, not each of them, but like for any of those channels, like what would that look like?
Yeah.
So for, I'll start with the CS, that one I remember really clearly.
So for customer success, like when they've onboarded a customer, we would then have like a, almost like a onboarding call with them.
And then I would build out like essentially just two slides of like, why you should join the community and what you can get.
And then the second one was like a QR code where it's like, you can join the community now, just take out your phone and scan it.
And then once you're in, you know, our community manager, community lead, Dani will welcome you.
So once they join, they get also that onboarding flow that I was talking about.
So it immediately seems like.
Like, you know, we're welcoming them.
And that's kind of like that one float.
Another thing is the sales piece.
So for the sales piece, right, we used both Apollo for like sequencing, but then also everything was routed through like HubSpot and Salesforce.
I think we used both for some reason.

But yeah, essentially, like once the deal was either like stuck or about to close, we also give enablement material to our sales team to essentially like welcome them and invite them into the community.
And then what was the last one that you asked me?
Oh, yeah.
How did people go from, I guess, like other pieces of content into the community?
So I think I remember telling you, you know, when we get customer speakers, right, into the conversion secrets, those are small online sessions, we then create like a written playbook as well.
So underneath the written playbook, there's a CTA on the website that says like, hey, if you want to hear other playbooks similar to this one, and if you want to contribute.
Come into the community because we're going to share like tidbits there.
And then if you want to be a contributor, like we would welcome that as well.
So there's that piece there.
That makes sense.
So it sounds like it's a lot of kind of like these other surfaces already exist and you're kind of thinking of how to essentially like create an impression for the community there.
And then, yeah, and then I guess just like kind of getting them to add that to whatever they're doing already.
Was there anything that was like very net new from your time there?
I mean, I guess for either like getting people into the community or for engagement that like felt maybe like the most substantial?
Yeah, I think one really fun thing.
mean, there was two, but they're kind of in the same bucket because one of our...
Biggest metrics company-wide was around like customer activation.
So a lot of customers, like, again, like when they buy Mutiny, they're all happy about it.
But sometimes like the barrier to learn it on the entry is kind of high.
So one campaign that we ran through the community was what we called March Madness.
So very similar to, you know, like the basketball, March Madness.
We put customers in brackets and essentially they had to run a conversion play using the product and then submit that to us.
And then we kind of like made them go from, I guess, like elimination rounds.
So people would get eliminated and go against another person.
And eventually there was going to be like the top two who would like face it off.
And then they would have to like pitch essentially their conversion play to us.
And that was also an event that we ran through the community.
So people can sign up even if they're not part of like the customer roster, but they can like, you know, vote for the person that they like.
And then afterwards, there was like a whole social brand play as well, where we made the customers into almost like, you know.
Those player cards, those sports playing cards, that was something that we shared online that the customers really loved because they're like, oh, that's so cool.
We then also mailed a physical copy of that card out to them.
then afterwards, I think the final winner, they got like this really cool prize, which was either like $1,000 in cash where it might have been like flying them to a conference.
I don't remember which one it was because we did another similar like, I guess, activation event that was the other.
So it was either flying them to a really cool conference or the $1,000 in cash.
Yeah, no, that sounds awesome.
I mean, it is like interesting, like the shape of these communities.
And I mean, it does feel like the community we're building is like quite different in a lot of ways.
It's like, it's almost like a community for its own sake instead of a community kind of very tightly coupled with like a product or a...
So...
So...
Or a service.
Like it does feel like almost like its own company.
So yeah, so that's interesting.
And then I guess at Apollo, so it's like a little bit more coupled with the product.
Were the metrics very different that you're trying to drive because of that?
Yeah, so I would say like from a community standpoint, it was somewhat similar.
So we also measure growth.
We also measure like engagement.
And like weekly active members.
But I think like when it comes to tying it back to the business metrics, was a little bit different.
Because from Mutiny, it was around like net pipeline generated from community activities and the conversion rate from that.
But then at Apollo, there's actually, it's a little bit different because it's more around like net retention rate and expansion.
Because it's, you know, it's based around mostly our customers.
So I think that would be the major difference.
And because like the community is very focused on how to use Apollo.
Like people.
people.
Google actually do use Apollo, whether you have a free plan or, you know, a paid plan.
So that's really the major difference there.
Yeah.
Yeah, I mean, the retention makes sense.

I mean, how do you guys deal with like, I guess the community can help retention, but it's hard to kind of like isolate the effect of like educational material, you know, because at the end of the day, I mean, if I can't use the product because it's clunky or my emails go to spam, like the data sucks.
You know, like, but you don't really have any control over that.
So how did, yeah, like, how did people like attribute or kind of assign like some sort of impact to the stuff in the community?
So what we do is a cohort analysis.
And of course, it's not like a direct causing effect, but there is correlation.
So we look at the cohorts of people who are in the community and the cohorts of people who are not in the community.
And we look at like, what's the difference in net retention?
information.
Thank
So the overall, like, I guess, expansion revenue from that.
So we've noticed that people who are in the community, they're almost like two to three times more likely to retain than people who are not in the community.
And another thing is that people who are in the community, like there is a substantial increase in like the actual expansion revenue that essentially closed from the community members.
